label: computes
description: Every Greek is a partial derivative of the option price — computing how Delta changes with the underlying (Gamma) or how a multi-leg portfolio s risk decomposes requires repeated application of the chain rule.

label: extended by
description: Ito s Lemma is the stochastic generalisation of the chain rule — it adds the correction term (1/2)sigma^2 S^2 f_{SS} dt that arises because (dW)^2 = dt, making it the essential tool for differentiating functions of stochastic processes.

label: includes
description: The chain rule is one of the core differentiation rules — it handles composite functions and is essential for differentiating any function where one quantity depends on another through an intermediate variable.

label: inverse of
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus establishes that differentiation and integration are inverse operations — differentiating the integral of a function recovers the original function, making differentiation rules the mirror image of integration.

label: inverse of
description: Integration is the reverse operation of differentiation — the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus states that the integral of a derivative recovers the original function, and every integration technique has a corresponding differentiation rule it reverses.

label: applied to
description: The derivatives of ln(x) and e^x are among the most important differentiation rules — they appear whenever continuous compounding, log-returns, or the lognormal model are differentiated.

label: generalised by
description: Partial differentiation extends single-variable differentiation to functions of several variables — the same power, product, chain, and quotient rules apply, but each derivative is taken with respect to one variable while holding the others constant.

label: foundation of
description: A Taylor series is built from repeated differentiation — the nth term is the nth derivative evaluated at the expansion point, so mastery of differentiation rules is a prerequisite for constructing any Taylor approximation.

label: applied in
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price with respect to its inputs — computing Delta, Gamma, Theta, and Vega requires direct application of the chain rule, product rule, and differentiation of the normal CDF.

label: applied in
description: Part 1 of the FTC gives the key identity F_X’(x) = f_X(x): the derivative of the CDF is the PDF. This relationship is used constantly when converting between cumulative probabilities and densities in option pricing and risk measurement.

label: foundation of
description: Part 2 of the FTC is what makes a PDF useful — the probability P(a < X < b) equals the integral of the density from a to b, evaluated as the antiderivative difference F(b) − F(a).

label: applied in
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is universal across all European options — the terminal condition (payoff) and boundary conditions are what select the unique pricing solution for each specific contract type.

label: determines
description: The terminal condition V(S,T) = max(S-K,0) for a call or max(K-S,0) for a put is the boundary condition that, combined with the Black-Scholes PDE, yields the closed-form European option price.

label: applied in
description: Taylor series can be integrated term by term within their radius of convergence, and many integral representations of special functions (such as the error function used in option pricing) are derived by integrating a Taylor expansion.

label: foundation of
description: The CDF is defined as the integral of the probability density function from minus infinity to x — computing any probability from a continuous distribution is a direct application of definite integration.

label: applied in
description: A valid probability density must integrate to one over its support, and every probability calculation involves integrating the PDF over an interval — substitution and by-parts are routinely needed for non-trivial densities.

label: used in
description: The Taylor expansion is itself a lemma — a proven result invoked as a building block inside larger proofs, such as deriving the Greeks or the Itô correction term.

label: proves
description: The variance addition lemma for independent random variables — Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) — is the foundational step behind the square-root rule for scaling volatility.

label: exemplified by
description: Itô's Lemma is the most important lemma in quantitative finance — a proven stepping-stone result that is invoked every time a function of a stochastic process needs to be differentiated.

label: extends
description: Continuous compounding A = Pe^{rt} is the limiting case of discrete compounding — the exponential function provides the natural framework for discounting and present value calculations in continuous time.

label: foundation of
description: The lognormal distribution arises because if ln(S) is normally distributed then S = e^X is lognormal — the entire model rests on the inverse relationship between log and exponential functions.

label: defines
description: Log-returns r_t = ln(S_t/S_{t-1}) are the standard return measure in quantitative finance because the logarithm converts multiplicative price ratios into additive quantities that are easier to model statistically.

label: classifies
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is a parabolic partial differential equation — its discriminant B^2 - 4AC = 0 because there is no u_yy term. The parabolic classification guarantees that option prices are smooth even when payoffs have kinks.

label: classifies
description: The Fokker-Planck (Kolmogorov forward) equation is a parabolic PDE — it describes the time evolution of transition probability densities for diffusion processes, and its parabolic nature ensures these densities smooth out over time.

label: prerequisite for
description: Initial and boundary conditions constrain solutions to PDEs, which are equations expressed in terms of partial derivatives — understanding partial differentiation is essential before specifying or interpreting these constraints.

label: foundation of
description: Parabolic PDEs are second-order partial differential equations — classifying and solving them requires fluency in partial derivatives, including the second-order terms u_xx, u_xy, and u_yy that enter the discriminant.

label: used in
description: The similarity solution method relies on computing partial derivatives of the ansatz p = t^a f(xi) with respect to both t and y using the chain rule for composite functions — partial differentiation is the mechanical core of the technique.

label: foundation of
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is expressed entirely in terms of partial derivatives of the option price with respect to the underlying price, time, and volatility — without partial differentiation the equation cannot be stated or solved.

label: applied in
description: Each Greek is a partial derivative of the option price: Delta = dV/dS, Gamma = d2V/dS2, Theta = dV/dt, Vega = dV/dsigma — computing and interpreting them requires fluency in partial differentiation.

label: derives
description: The similarity exponent b = 1/2 that emerges from dimensional consistency is exactly the square-root-of-time scaling — volatility scales as sigma*sqrt(T) because the underlying diffusion equation is self-similar with this exponent.

label: solves
description: Applying the similarity ansatz to the Fokker-Planck equation for free Brownian motion yields the Gaussian transition density — the fundamental solution that underpins all diffusion-based pricing models.

label: applied in
description: The continuous-time limit derivation uses a Taylor expansion of the option value in powers of delta-t to extract the Black-Scholes PDE from the discrete binomial pricing formula.

label: motivates
description: Itô's Lemma is the stochastic calculus equivalent of a Taylor expansion — it tells you how a function of a random process changes, using the same first- and second-derivative structure. The proof of Itô’s Lemma proceeds by Taylor-expanding f(S, t) to second order and substituting (dW)² = dt — the Taylor expansion is not merely motivational but the actual derivation technique.

label: requires
description: Pricing an American option requires evaluating the early exercise decision at every node — the option price is the maximum of the intrinsic value and the continuation value.

label: extends
description: An American option is worth at least as much as its European counterpart — the early-exercise premium is the additional value from the right to exercise before expiry.

label: compares
description: The comparison card contrasts the pricing methodologies, exercise rights, and put-call parity relationships that distinguish American from European options.

label: compares
description: The comparison card highlights that European options admit closed-form Black-Scholes prices and exact put-call parity, serving as the analytically tractable baseline.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity holds exactly for European options but only as inequality bounds for American options — the early-exercise right breaks the strict equality.

label: extended by
description: American option pricing uses backward induction with an additional early-exercise check at each node — the algorithm compares the continuation value against the immediate exercise payoff and keeps the maximum.

label: computes
description: At each node of the backward pass, the hedge ratio (delta) is computed as the ratio of the change in option value to the change in stock price across the two successor nodes, giving the exact number of shares needed to replicate the option.

label: prices
description: Backward induction on a binomial tree prices European options by discounting terminal payoffs step-by-step to the present — as the number of steps grows, the price converges to the Black-Scholes closed form.

label: implements
description: Each backward step applies the one-period risk-neutral pricing formula — discounting the probability-weighted average of successor values at the risk-free rate — making backward induction a discrete implementation of risk-neutral valuation.

label: converges to
description: As the number of time steps N tends to infinity, the binomial tree converges to the continuous-time Black-Scholes model — this is the Cox-Ross-Rubinstein convergence result.

label: derives
description: The hedge ratio (delta) emerges naturally within the binomial framework as the ratio of the spread in option values to the spread in stock prices at each node.

label: demonstrates
description: The binomial model is the simplest demonstration of risk-neutral pricing — the real-world probability cancels out and only the risk-neutral probability p-prime enters the pricing formula.

label: enables
description: Black-Scholes shows that a continuously rebalanced Delta hedge eliminates all price risk, which is precisely the argument used to derive the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: prices
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form prices for European calls and puts — it is the standard analytical pricing tool for European-style options. The closed-form Black–Scholes formula applies only to European-style options because it assumes the terminal payoff is the sole boundary condition — American options introduce a free boundary (early exercise) that the PDE cannot handle without numerical methods.

label: inverted to get
description: Implied volatility is found by running Black-Scholes in reverse: given a market price for an option, you solve numerically for the volatility that makes the formula match.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form expressions for Delta, Gamma, Vega, Theta and Rho — the Greeks are simply partial derivatives of the Black-Scholes price with respect to each input.

label: assumed by
description: The Black-Scholes equation is derived under the assumption of a complete market — continuous trading in the stock and bond is sufficient to replicate any European payoff, yielding a unique price.

label: enables
description: Market completeness is the theoretical guarantee that delta hedging works: because every contingent claim can be replicated, the hedging portfolio perfectly offsets the option exposure.

label: guarantees uniqueness of
description: In a complete market the risk-neutral measure is unique — completeness plus no-arbitrage together ensure there is exactly one pricing measure, which is the second Fundamental Theorem of Asset Pricing.

label: derives
description: Taking the continuous-time limit of the binomial model produces the Black-Scholes PDE — this card shows how the discrete one-step formula converges to the continuous PDE as the time step shrinks to zero.

label: informed by
description: Theta and the cost of carry determine whether early exercise is worthwhile — when Theta decay exceeds the interest earned on the intrinsic value, continuing to hold is optimal.

label: driven by
description: The core trade-off in early exercise is whether receiving cash now and earning interest outweighs the option's remaining time value — a direct application of the time value of money.

label: valued via
description: European option prices equal the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — this expectation is the defining calculation for European-style derivatives.

label: extends
description: The Heston model generalises Black-Scholes by replacing the constant-volatility assumption with a stochastic variance process — Black-Scholes is recovered as the special case where the vol of vol is zero. Black–Scholes is recovered as the special case of the Heston model when the vol-of-vol σ_v → 0 and the initial variance equals θ — Heston nests BS as a degenerate limit, providing a consistency check on the more general framework.

label: violates
description: The Heston model introduces stochastic volatility as a second source of randomness that cannot be hedged with the stock alone, making the market incomplete and requiring an additional instrument or a market price of volatility risk.

label: explains
description: The Heston model's stochastic volatility and negative correlation between returns and variance reproduce the implied volatility smile and skew that Black-Scholes' flat volatility assumption cannot capture.

label: priced via
description: Although the Heston model has a semi-analytical solution for European options, exotic and path-dependent derivatives under Heston are typically priced by Monte Carlo simulation of the two correlated SDEs.

label: generalises
description: GBM assumes constant volatility, while the Heston model replaces this with a mean-reverting stochastic variance process — the asset price SDE is augmented with a second coupled SDE for the variance.

label: uses
description: The Heston model's variance process is a mean-reverting square-root diffusion (CIR process) — the speed of mean reversion κ and long-run variance θ control how quickly volatility returns to its equilibrium after a shock.

label: reveals
description: The volatility smile and skew observed in implied volatility across strikes expose the failure of Black-Scholes' constant-volatility assumption — the market prices in fat tails and jump risk that the model ignores.

label: justified by
description: Delta hedging constructs the riskless portfolio whose existence the no-arbitrage principle requires — without a perfect hedge, the argument that the portfolio must earn exactly r collapses.

label: implies
description: Put-call parity is a direct consequence of no-arbitrage: if the relationship between a call, a put, the stock, and a bond were violated, a trader could construct a risk-free profit from the mispricing.

label: foundation of
description: The no-arbitrage condition guarantees the existence of a risk-neutral measure under which all discounted asset prices are martingales — this is the First Fundamental Theorem of Asset Pricing.

label: derived via
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price; a Taylor expansion in those derivatives gives a local approximation of how the portfolio P&L changes with small moves in price, time, and volatility.

label: parameterises
description: Delta is the hedge ratio — it tells you exactly how many units of the underlying to hold in order to offset the option's directional exposure. Gamma measures how fast Delta changes with the underlying price — a high-Gamma position requires more frequent rebalancing of the delta hedge, making Gamma the key Greek for monitoring hedging cost and exposure between rebalances.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity forces the Delta of a put and the Delta of the equivalent call to sum to one (or negative one), providing a quick sanity check on any Greeks calculation.

label: framework for
description: The Black-Scholes price equals the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — risk-neutral pricing is the theoretical foundation that justifies the formula.

label: foundation of
description: Risk-neutral pricing provides the one-step discounted expectation formula that is the starting point for the continuous-time limit derivation; the risk-neutral probability is what causes the drift to cancel.

label: exposes limitation of
description: The existence of the smile directly contradicts Black-Scholes' constant-volatility assumption — if the model were exact, implied volatility would be flat across all strikes.

label: reproduced by
description: The Heston model's stochastic volatility and correlation between returns and variance generate an implied volatility smile from first principles, providing a theoretically grounded alternative to strike-by-strike fitting.

label: maps across strikes
description: The volatility smile is the graph of implied volatility as a function of strike price — each point on the smile is an implied volatility extracted from a single option's market price via the Black-Scholes formula.

label: evidences
description: The volatility smile is empirical proof that real return distributions have fatter tails and asymmetry compared to the lognormal — it is one of the most visible model limitations in practice.

label: derived from
description: APT is built on the no-arbitrage principle — if an asset's expected return does not equal the sum of its factor risk premia, a portfolio of many assets can be constructed to earn riskless profit, which the market eliminates.

label: generalises
description: APT generalises CAPM by allowing multiple systematic risk factors rather than a single market factor — CAPM is the special case of APT where the only priced factor is the market portfolio.

label: alternative to
description: While MPT and CAPM derive asset pricing from equilibrium assumptions about investor behaviour, APT reaches similar conclusions using only the weaker no-arbitrage condition, making it a more general alternative framework.

label: models
description: CAPM models the expected return of an asset as a linear function of its systematic risk — the return on investment is the quantity that CAPM predicts.

label: depends on
description: Beta is the ratio of the covariance between asset and market returns to market variance — computing beta requires estimating the correlation between the asset and the market portfolio.

label: computed from
description: Beta equals Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m) — the market variance in the denominator normalises the covariance to produce a unitless sensitivity measure.

label: extends
description: The Fama–French model adds size (SMB) and value (HML) factors to the single market factor of CAPM, explaining cross-sectional return patterns that CAPM alone attributes incorrectly to alpha.

label: estimated via
description: Factor loadings in the Fama–French model are estimated by regressing excess returns on the three factor time series — the regression minimises the variance of the residual to find the best-fitting sensitivities.

label: foundation of
description: CAPM is derived from Modern Portfolio Theory by asking what expected returns must hold in equilibrium when all investors hold mean-variance efficient portfolios — it is the single-factor equilibrium result of MPT.

label: computed via
description: Portfolio variance is the quadratic form w'Σw and expected return is the dot product w'μ — both are matrix multiplications, making linear algebra the computational engine of portfolio optimisation.

label: feeds into
description: The portfolio variance from MPT is the key input to parametric VaR — once mean-variance weights are chosen, the portfolio standard deviation is plugged directly into the VaR formula to quantify downside risk.

label: depends on
description: Portfolio variance is determined by the pairwise correlations between assets — the off-diagonal terms of the covariance matrix drive the diversification benefit that is the central result of MPT.

label: minimises
description: MPT defines risk as portfolio variance and seeks the weight vector that minimises it for a given target return — variance is the objective function at the heart of mean-variance optimisation.

label: input to
description: Daily returns calculated from asset prices are the raw data whose mean and standard deviation characterise the asset's drift and volatility.

label: visualises
description: The Security Market Line is the graphical representation of the CAPM equation — it plots expected return against beta, making the linear risk-return trade-off visually explicit.

label: related to
description: Both the Sharpe Ratio and CAPM use the risk-free rate as a baseline — CAPM prices expected return via systematic risk, while the Sharpe Ratio evaluates realised performance per unit of total risk.

label: optimised by
description: The tangency portfolio on the efficient frontier is the portfolio that maximises the Sharpe Ratio — mean-variance optimisation and Sharpe maximisation are equivalent when a risk-free asset is available.

label: uses
description: The numerator of the Sharpe Ratio is excess return — the portfolio return minus the risk-free rate — so computing the Sharpe Ratio begins with calculating returns.

label: normalised by
description: The denominator of the Sharpe Ratio is the standard deviation of portfolio returns — it normalises excess return by volatility to produce a risk-adjusted performance measure.

label: generalised by
description: The time value of money uses a single discount rate, but the yield curve generalises this to a different rate for every maturity — each cash flow is discounted at its own spot rate rather than one flat rate.

label: analysed via
description: PCA on the covariance matrix of yield curve changes decomposes movements into eigenvectors (level, slope, curvature) — typically three factors explain over 95% of all variation in interest rates.

label: bootstrapped via
description: Constructing the yield curve from coupon bond prices requires solving a triangular system of linear equations — each new maturity adds one equation and one unknown spot rate.

label: requires
description: The Cholesky decomposition A = LLᵀ is only defined for symmetric, positive-definite matrices — the symmetry of the covariance matrix is a prerequisite for the factorisation.

label: enables
description: Multi-asset Monte Carlo simulation uses the Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix to convert independent random draws into correctly correlated shocks — without it, simulated paths would ignore diversification.

label: decomposes
description: The Cholesky factorisation splits the correlation matrix into a lower triangular matrix and its transpose — this is the practical mechanism for imposing a correlation structure on simulated random variables.

label: gates
description: A matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is non-zero — checking the determinant is the first step before attempting to invert a covariance matrix for portfolio optimisation.

label: underlies
description: Portfolio variance w^T Sigma w is a quadratic form built from dot products of weight and return vectors — the entire mean-variance optimisation framework rests on this inner product structure.

label: building block of
description: Matrix multiplication is defined as a collection of dot products — the (i,j) entry of AB is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B.

label: defines
description: Correlation is a normalised dot product of de-meaned return vectors — the geometric interpretation cos(theta) explains why correlation is bounded between -1 and +1 via the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

label: product gives
description: The determinant of a matrix equals the product of its eigenvalues — a zero eigenvalue means the matrix is singular, directly linking spectral analysis to invertibility.

label: simplified by
description: Symmetric matrices guarantee real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors — this is why covariance matrices, which are always symmetric, admit clean PCA decompositions with interpretable risk factors.

label: decomposes
description: Each eigenvalue of the covariance matrix represents the variance explained by its corresponding eigenvector — the total variance is the sum of all eigenvalues, partitioning risk across independent factors.

label: solves
description: The solution to Ax = b is x = A⁻¹b — computing the matrix inverse is one method for solving systems of linear equations, used in regression and portfolio optimisation.

label: prerequisite for
description: Computing the determinant of larger matrices via cofactor expansion requires repeated multiplication of matrix elements — matrix arithmetic is the foundation on which the determinant is built.

label: defines
description: The inverse is defined by the product AA⁻¹ = I — verifying and computing an inverse requires matrix multiplication.

label: used in
description: Portfolio VaR for multiple assets requires the matrix calculation w'Σw to compute portfolio variance — matrix multiplication is the computational tool that combines individual volatilities with their correlations.

label: computes
description: The correlation matrix is built and manipulated via matrix operations — portfolio risk, principal component analysis, and factor models all rely on multiplying weight vectors by covariance or correlation matrices.

label: required for
description: The Cholesky decomposition A = LL^T exists if and only if A is positive definite — this is the standard factorisation used to generate correlated random variables in Monte Carlo simulation.

label: characterised by
description: A symmetric matrix is positive definite if and only if all its eigenvalues are strictly positive — checking the eigenvalue spectrum is the most reliable test for valid covariance matrices.

label: enables
description: Monte Carlo simulation of correlated assets requires Cholesky-decomposing the covariance matrix, which is only possible when it is positive definite — a rank-deficient or indefinite matrix breaks the simulation.

label: equivalent to
description: A square matrix has full rank if and only if its determinant is non-zero — rank and the determinant are two different lenses on the same question of whether a matrix is singular.

label: counted via
description: The rank of a matrix equals the number of non-zero eigenvalues — a zero eigenvalue signals a rank deficiency, which in PCA means that principal component explains no variance.

label: gates
description: A square matrix is invertible if and only if it has full rank — rank deficiency in a covariance matrix means the inverse does not exist and portfolio optimisation cannot proceed without regularisation.

label: classifies
description: The rank of the coefficient matrix determines whether a linear system has a unique solution (full rank), infinitely many solutions, or no solution — it is the central diagnostic for solvability.

label: checked via
description: The determinant determines whether a system has a unique solution (det ≠ 0), no solution, or infinitely many solutions — it is the first diagnostic tool for any linear system.

label: complements
description: While the trace gives the sum of eigenvalues, the determinant gives their product — together they provide scalar summaries of a matrix that appear in characteristic polynomial coefficients.

label: equals sum of
description: The trace of a matrix equals the sum of its eigenvalues — this identity connects a simple diagonal sum to the spectral decomposition used in PCA and risk factor analysis.

label: used in
description: The portfolio variance formula wᵀΣw requires the transpose operation to convert the weight vector into a row — the transpose is integral to every quadratic form in matrix algebra.

label: updates
description: Bayes' Theorem updates your probability distribution as new information arrives; the expected value computed from that updated distribution is your revised estimate of fair value.

label: prices via
description: The binomial tree model prices American options by working backwards through a recombining tree — at each node the number of up-moves follows a binomial distribution, and early exercise is checked at every step.

label: applied in
description: The terminal stock price distribution in an N-step recombining binomial tree follows a binomial distribution with parameters N and p-prime, connecting discrete probability theory directly to option pricing.

label: defines mean of
description: The expected value of a binomial random variable is np — this simple formula gives the average number of up-moves in a binomial tree, which determines the expected terminal stock price.

label: converges to
description: By the Central Limit Theorem, the binomial distribution approaches the normal distribution as n grows — this convergence is why the binomial tree model reproduces Black-Scholes prices in the continuous limit.

label: limiting case of
description: When the number of trials n is large and the probability p is small, the binomial distribution converges to the Poisson — this limit is used in credit risk to model rare default events in large portfolios.

label: illustrates
description: The binomial distribution is the textbook illustration of the CLT — the sum of n independent Bernoulli trials becomes approximately normal as n grows, which is exactly the convergence that turns a binomial tree into Black-Scholes.

label: counts steps of
description: Each step of a discrete random walk is an independent up-or-down trial — the total number of up-moves after n steps follows a binomial distribution B(n, p).

label: built from
description: The chi-squared distribution is the sum of squared standard normals — it arises naturally when testing whether the variance of normally distributed returns has changed.

label: component of
description: The t-distribution is constructed as a standard normal divided by the square root of a chi-squared divided by its degrees of freedom — the two distributions are algebraically inseparable in small-sample inference.

label: distribution of
description: The Jarque-Bera test statistic follows a chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom under the null hypothesis of normality — the chi-squared CDF provides the p-value used to accept or reject.

label: tests
description: The chi-squared test statistic (n−1)s²/σ₀² follows a chi-squared distribution under the null hypothesis — this is the standard method for testing whether realised volatility matches a hypothesised value.

label: applied in
description: The Black-Scholes formula uses Φ(d₁) and Φ(d₂) — the cumulative normal distribution function — to compute the option price as a weighted combination of probabilities.

label: integrates
description: The CDF is the integral of the PDF — F(x) accumulates the density from −∞ to x, and conversely the PDF is the derivative of the CDF.

label: inverted via
description: The inverse CDF (quantile function) transforms a uniform U(0,1) draw into a sample from any target distribution — this inverse transform method is the bridge between uniform random numbers and Monte Carlo simulation.

label: defines quantile of
description: Value at Risk is defined as a quantile of the loss distribution — it is the inverse CDF evaluated at the confidence level, making the CDF the mathematical object that directly determines VaR.

label: discounted by
description: A fair price equals the expected future payoff discounted back to today — combining expected value with time-value-of-money discounting is the core of risk-neutral option pricing.

label: mirrors
description: The survival probability e^{−λt} has the same exponential form as the continuous discount factor e^{−rt} — default intensity in credit risk plays an analogous role to the risk-free rate in time-value-of-money discounting.

label: inter-arrival times of
description: If events arrive as a Poisson process with rate λ, the waiting time between consecutive events follows an exponential distribution with the same rate — the two distributions are inseparable.

label: contrasts with
description: Fat-tailed distributions are defined by comparison with the normal — the normal's exponentially decaying tails assign negligibly small probabilities to extreme events, while fat-tailed distributions assign far greater probability, making the normal the baseline that fat tails violate.

label: motivates
description: The presence of fat tails in real return data is the single most important empirical fact driving model limitations — any model that assumes normality will systematically underestimate the frequency and severity of extreme losses.

label: undermines
description: Parametric VaR computed under a normal assumption understates tail losses because fat-tailed returns produce extreme quantiles that are far larger than the Gaussian z-score implies. Fat tails push more probability mass into extreme losses, so the true 99th-percentile loss exceeds the Gaussian estimate — switching from a normal to a fat-tailed model systematically increases VaR at high confidence levels.

label: assumed by
description: Black-Scholes assumes stock prices are lognormally distributed — the N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms in the formula are computed from the normal distribution of log-prices.

label: generalises
description: The MGF encodes all moments of a distribution — the first derivative at zero gives the expected value, and higher derivatives give higher moments, making it a compact summary of the entire moment sequence.

label: characterises
description: The normal distribution's MGF exp(μt + ½σ²t²) uniquely identifies it — this is the tool used to prove that the sum of independent normals is again normal, a result that underpins portfolio variance calculations.

label: extracts
description: The variance is recovered from the MGF via Var(X) = M''(0) − [M'(0)]² — the MGF packages the first and second moments needed to compute volatility into a single generating function.

label: N(d1), N(d2) from
description: The Black-Scholes formula contains N(d₁) and N(d₂), which are cumulative normal probabilities representing the option's Delta and the risk-neutral probability of finishing in the money. Black–Scholes assumes log-returns are normally distributed via the GBM model — this distributional assumption is distinct from the formula’s mechanical use of N(d₁) and N(d₂), and is the reason the model underprices tail risk.

label: exponentiates to
description: If log-returns are normally distributed then the price itself is lognormally distributed — the lognormal is simply the exponential of a normal random variable.

label: used in
description: Parametric VaR assumes returns are normally distributed, so the 1% or 5% loss quantile is simply the mean minus a fixed multiple of the standard deviation (1.645σ for 95%, 2.326σ for 99%). Parametric VaR assumes portfolio returns are normally distributed, placing all tail-risk information into the variance — this is a modelling assumption distinct from the normal distribution being used as a computational tool in VaR calculations.

label: fits
description: Standardised daily returns closely match the normal bell curve — this empirical observation is the justification for modelling returns as normally distributed random variables.

label: parameterised by
description: The normal distribution is fully described by its mean and variance — knowing these two parameters is enough to calculate any probability or quantile, including VaR.

label: increments of
description: Brownian motion is built from normal increments — each infinitesimal step dW is drawn from N(0, dt), so the normal distribution is the elementary building block of all continuous-time models.

label: addresses
description: Jump-diffusion models add Poisson-distributed jumps to GBM to capture the fat tails and sudden crashes that pure Brownian motion cannot explain — a direct response to the limitations of the normal model.

label: augments
description: In Merton's jump-diffusion model, a Poisson process is layered on top of Brownian motion — the continuous diffusion handles normal price moves while Poisson jumps capture sudden large moves.

label: computes
description: The expected value of a continuous random variable is defined as the integral of x weighted by the PDF — E[X] = ∫x f(x) dx — making the density function the essential ingredient for computing fair values.

label: exemplified by
description: The normal distribution's bell-shaped PDF is the most widely used density in finance — its specific functional form determines the probabilities that feed into Black-Scholes, VaR, and every Gaussian risk model.

label: example of
description: The Student's t-distribution is the most commonly used fat-tailed distribution in finance — its polynomial tail decay and tuneable degrees-of-freedom parameter make it a tractable replacement for the normal when modelling heavy-tailed returns.

label: generalises
description: The t-distribution has heavier tails than the normal and converges to it as degrees of freedom increase — with low ν it captures the excess kurtosis observed in real asset returns.

label: improves
description: Using the t-distribution instead of the normal for VaR calculations produces wider tail quantiles, giving more conservative and realistic loss estimates that account for fat tails in returns.

label: drives
description: Monte Carlo simulation begins with uniform U(0,1) random draws, which are transformed into the target distribution via the inverse CDF method — the uniform distribution is the raw input to every simulation.

label: defined via
description: Both VaR and Expected Shortfall are defined as functionals of the loss CDF — VaR is the quantile (inverse CDF) and ES is the average of quantiles beyond the VaR level, so the CDF is the mathematical foundation for evaluating coherence.

label: complements
description: EVT models the shape of the tail while coherence axioms govern how a risk measure aggregates tail losses — combining EVT-fitted tails with a coherent measure like Expected Shortfall produces robust extreme-risk estimates.

label: formalises
description: The coherence axioms formalise the shortcomings of naive risk measures — subadditivity failure in VaR is a specific, mathematically precise model limitation that coherent measures are designed to eliminate.

label: critiques
description: VaR violates the subadditivity axiom of coherence — merging portfolios can increase VaR, penalising diversification — which is the central motivation for replacing VaR with coherent alternatives like Expected Shortfall.

label: extends beyond
description: The normal distribution's exponentially decaying tails correspond to the Gumbel case (ξ = 0) of the GEV family — financial returns typically exhibit Fréchet-type tails (ξ > 0), which are heavier than the normal allows.

label: addresses
description: EVT directly confronts the fat-tail limitation of Gaussian risk models — by fitting only the extreme observations, it captures the heavy tails that the normal distribution misses.

label: improves tail of
description: EVT provides a statistically rigorous estimate of VaR at very high confidence levels (99.9%) by modelling the tail directly, rather than relying on the normal approximation that systematically underestimates extreme losses.

label: challenges
description: Real returns have fatter tails than the normal distribution predicts — extreme events like the 1987 crash occur far more often than the model allows, undermining VaR and Black-Scholes in the tails.

label: questions
description: The basic model assumes constant drift and volatility, but real markets exhibit volatility clustering and regime changes — motivating GARCH, stochastic volatility, and jump-diffusion models.

label: computes
description: Monte Carlo VaR simulates thousands of portfolio paths and reads the loss quantile directly from the resulting distribution, avoiding the normality assumption of parametric VaR.

label: exposes
description: Significant autocorrelation in squared returns reveals volatility clustering — a direct violation of the constant-volatility assumption in Black-Scholes and basic VaR models.

label: special case of
description: Autocorrelation is correlation applied to a time series and its own lagged values, rather than between two different variables — the same Pearson formula underpins both measures.

label: diagnoses
description: Testing for autocorrelation in returns is a key diagnostic — near-zero autocorrelation in raw returns supports market efficiency, while positive autocorrelation in squared returns confirms volatility clustering.

label: parameterises
description: Beta is the key input parameter to CAPM — estimating it from historical data is the essential step that turns the theoretical pricing equation into a practical tool for computing expected returns.

label: decomposed via
description: Beta can be decomposed as the product of the correlation between asset and market returns and the ratio of their standard deviations — separating the directional association from the relative volatility.

label: instance of
description: Estimating beta from a finite sample of returns is a parameter estimation problem — subject to sampling error, bias, and the choice of estimator (OLS, Bayesian shrinkage, or Blume adjustment).

label: normalised by
description: The OLS beta estimator divides the covariance of asset and market returns by the market variance — this normalisation converts a covariance into a unitless sensitivity measure.

label: proved via
description: The classic proof of the CLT uses moment generating functions — showing that the MGF of the standardised sum converges to the normal MGF establishes convergence in distribution to the Gaussian.

label: converges to
description: The CLT states that the standardised sum of i.i.d. random variables converges to the normal distribution — this is the fundamental reason the normal distribution is the default model for aggregate returns and portfolio losses.

label: justifies
description: Parametric VaR assumes portfolio losses are normally distributed — the CLT justifies this by guaranteeing that the sum of many independent risk factors is approximately normal, even when individual factors are not.

label: validates
description: The CLT guarantees that sample means are approximately normal for large n, which justifies constructing confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for estimated drift and volatility using normal quantiles.

label: feeds into
description: Portfolio VaR depends on the correlations between positions — in a multi-asset portfolio, low correlations reduce aggregate risk even when individual volatilities are unchanged.

label: calibrates
description: The annualised drift and volatility used in every pricing and risk model are estimated from historical daily returns — multiplying the mean by 252 and the standard deviation by √252.

label: tests for
description: The Jarque-Bera test is a formal hypothesis test for normality — it checks whether a sample's skewness and kurtosis are consistent with the normal distribution, which is the assumed return distribution in many pricing and risk models.

label: diagnoses
description: When the Jarque-Bera test rejects normality for a return series, it provides direct statistical evidence that Gaussian-based models (Black-Scholes, parametric VaR) may understate tail risk — motivating fat-tailed alternatives.

label: formalises
description: The Jarque-Bera test converts the descriptive observation that returns exhibit skewness and excess kurtosis into a formal statistical test with a p-value, quantifying how far the data deviate from normality.

label: explains
description: The volatility smile arises because the market prices in fatter tails than Black-Scholes assumes — kurtosis quantifies exactly this tail heaviness, and higher excess kurtosis in the risk-neutral distribution produces a more pronounced smile.

label: benchmarked against
description: The normal distribution serves as the reference point for kurtosis — its kurtosis of exactly 3 (excess kurtosis 0) is the baseline, and deviations from this value indicate heavier or lighter tails than the Gaussian model assumes.

label: feeds into
description: High kurtosis means extreme losses occur more frequently than a normal model predicts — VaR estimates that ignore excess kurtosis systematically understate tail risk, which is why practitioners adjust for fat tails when calibrating loss distributions.

label: contrasted in
description: Mean returns scale linearly with time while volatility scales with the square root — this asymmetry explains why drift dominates at long horizons but is invisible at short ones.

label: explains
description: Negative skewness in equity returns is the physical-measure counterpart of the implied volatility skew — the market prices out-of-the-money puts higher because large downward moves are more probable than a symmetric model predicts.

label: absent from
description: The normal distribution has zero skewness by symmetry — any observed skewness in return data is direct evidence that the Gaussian assumption is inadequate for tail risk estimation.

label: characterises
description: Skewness is one of the key stylised facts of financial returns — equity indices typically show negative skewness, meaning crash risk is more pronounced than a symmetric distribution would imply.

label: scales
description: The square-root rule is used to convert VaR between time horizons — a 1-day VaR is scaled to a 10-day VaR by multiplying by √10, as required by Basel regulatory standards.

label: explains
description: Because volatility grows as √t while drift grows as t, the signal-to-noise ratio improves with horizon length — the square-root rule is the quantitative reason why short-term forecasting is so difficult.

label: motivates
description: The empirically observed fat tails, excess kurtosis, and occasional extreme moves in financial returns are precisely the phenomena that EVT is designed to model — stylised facts of returns provide the motivation for using EVT.

label: motivates
description: Empirical return distributions exhibit fat tails, skewness, and volatility clustering that deviate from the normal assumption — these observed properties are the primary evidence motivating more sophisticated risk models.

label: extended by
description: Variance measures dispersion at a single point in time, while autocorrelation extends this to measure how dispersion persists across time — connecting static risk measurement to dynamic volatility behaviour.

label: foundation of
description: Kurtosis is defined as the fourth moment divided by the square of the variance — without understanding variance as the second central moment, the standardisation that makes kurtosis comparable across distributions is meaningless.

label: extended by
description: Variance captures the spread of a distribution (second moment) while skewness captures its asymmetry (third moment) — both are needed to fully describe the shape of a return distribution beyond its centre.

label: underpins
description: The square-root rule follows directly from the variance addition property for independent random variables — total variance equals the sum of individual variances, so total standard deviation grows as √n.

label: motivates
description: The empirical observation that volatility clusters in time is the primary motivation for stochastic volatility models like Heston — the model's mean-reverting variance process directly captures the persistence of high- and low-volatility regimes.

label: invalidates assumptions of
description: Volatility clustering means that the constant-variance assumption behind parametric VaR is violated — during turbulent periods the true conditional volatility is much higher than the unconditional estimate, causing VaR to understate risk precisely when it matters most.

label: refines
description: While the statistical properties card establishes that standardised returns are approximately normal, volatility clustering reveals that the variance used for standardisation is itself time-varying — a deeper stylised fact that simple summary statistics miss.

label: extends
description: Variance and standard deviation treat volatility as a fixed number, but volatility clustering shows that it changes over time — motivating conditional variance models like GARCH that update the volatility estimate as new data arrives.

label: foundation of
description: The backward Kolmogorov equation is the skeleton of the Black-Scholes PDE — adding risk-neutral drift rS dV/dS and discounting -rV to the pure diffusion equation yields the full Black-Scholes equation.

label: driven by
description: The diffusion coefficient c enters the backward Kolmogorov equation as the c² term multiplying the second spatial derivative — it controls the rate at which uncertainty in the terminal payoff propagates backward in time.

label: adjoint of
description: The backward Kolmogorov equation and the Fokker-Planck (forward Kolmogorov) equation are adjoint PDEs — the forward equation evolves the probability density forward in time, while the backward equation evolves expected payoffs backward from a terminal condition.

label: underlies
description: Because the underlying asset follows a Brownian-driven process, option prices are stochastic and their sensitivities (the Greeks) describe how that randomness flows through to the portfolio.

label: extended to
description: Geometric Brownian Motion replaces the additive increments of standard Brownian Motion with multiplicative ones, ensuring the stock price can never go negative. Brownian motion supplies the dW noise term in the GBM stochastic differential equation dS = μS dt + σS dW — it is the mechanical source of randomness, distinct from the mathematical generalisation that GBM represents.

label: example of
description: Standard Brownian motion is the canonical continuous-time martingale — its independent, zero-mean increments ensure that the conditional expectation of any future value equals the current value.

label: appears in
description: The diffusion coefficient σS enters the Black-Scholes PDE as the ½σ²S² term multiplying the second derivative — it is the source of the Gamma risk that delta hedging must manage.

label: multiplies
description: The diffusion coefficient scales the Brownian increment dW — without it, the raw Wiener process has unit variance per unit time, so the diffusion coefficient is what calibrates the noise to match observed market volatility.

label: scales by
description: The diffusion coefficient σS combines the volatility parameter σ with the current price S — volatility sets the percentage scale of randomness, while the diffusion coefficient converts it into absolute price units.

label: implements
description: Monte Carlo simulation works by repeatedly applying the discrete random walk formula with fresh random draws — each application of the formula generates one step of one simulated price path.

label: becomes
description: The discrete random walk is the finite-step approximation that becomes the continuous SDE dS = μS dt + σS dX as the time step δt → 0.

label: replaced under
description: Under the risk-neutral measure the drift coefficient changes from μS to rS — this replacement is the mathematical content of risk-neutral pricing and the reason option prices do not depend on expected returns.

label: paired with
description: The drift and diffusion coefficients are the two components of any SDE — the drift controls the deterministic trend while the diffusion controls the random fluctuations, and together they fully specify the process.

label: formalises
description: The drift coefficient a(S) = μS is the continuous-time formalisation of the drift parameter μ — it converts the annualised expected return into an instantaneous rate of price change proportional to the current level.

label: requires
description: The Fokker-Planck (forward Kolmogorov) equation requires an initial condition — typically a delta function at the starting point — to select the unique transition probability density from its infinitely many solutions.

label: dual of
description: The Fokker-Planck equation and the Black-Scholes PDE are adjoint operators — Fokker-Planck evolves the density forward in time while Black-Scholes evolves the option price backward, both governed by the same drift and diffusion.

label: derives
description: Solving the Fokker-Planck equation for GBM confirms that the terminal stock price is lognormally distributed — the equation provides the rigorous proof of the distributional assumption underlying Black-Scholes.

label: driven by
description: The diffusion coefficient b(X) enters the Fokker-Planck equation as the second-order term ½∂²(b²p)/∂x² — it determines how quickly the probability density spreads out over time.

label: driven by
description: The drift coefficient a(X) enters the Fokker-Planck equation as the first-order term −∂(ap)/∂x — it determines the direction in which the probability density shifts over time.

label: is a
description: The Gaussian transition density is a normal distribution whose mean is the starting position and whose variance grows linearly with the time interval — the central limit theorem guarantees this Gaussian shape for any diffusion built from independent increments.

label: exhibits
description: The standard deviation of the transition density scales as the square root of elapsed time — the same √t scaling that governs how volatility converts between time horizons in finance.

label: characterises
description: The Gaussian transition density completely characterises Brownian motion — it gives the exact probability of the process reaching any point at any future time, encoding the independent Gaussian increments property.

label: solves
description: The Gaussian transition density is the fundamental solution (Green's function) of the Fokker-Planck equation with zero drift and constant diffusion — it satisfies the PDE with a delta-function initial condition.

label: models stock price for
description: Black-Scholes assumes the stock price follows GBM — this single assumption leads directly to the lognormal distribution of returns and the closed-form pricing formula.

label: emerges from
description: Geometric Brownian Motion is the continuous-time stock-price model that the binomial tree converges to in the limit — the CRR up/down parameters are chosen precisely so that this convergence holds.

label: produces
description: GBM implies that log-prices are normally distributed, so the price itself follows a lognormal distribution — this is why asset prices are always positive and right-skewed. The analytical solution of the GBM SDE is S_T = S₀ exp((μ − ½σ²)T + σW_T), which is a lognormal random variable — the lognormal distribution provides both the closed-form solution and the transition density needed for option pricing.

label: becomes under Q
description: Under the real-world measure, GBM with drift μ ≠ r is not a martingale after discounting — but under the risk-neutral measure Q, the discounted GBM stock price becomes a martingale, which is the key insight enabling derivative pricing.

label: derives
description: Applying Itô's Lemma to the option price as a function of a GBM stock price, then invoking the no-arbitrage condition, yields the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: solves
description: Itô's Lemma is used to derive the explicit solution for GBM — applying it to ln(S) converts the stochastic differential equation into a simple integral that gives S(t) in closed form. Applying Itô’s Lemma to ln(S) reveals the −½σ² convexity correction in the drift of log-returns — this correction is why E[S_T] = S₀ e^{μT} but the median is S₀ e^{(μ−½σ²)T}, a distinction central to risk management.

label: foundation of
description: Risk-neutral pricing rests on the martingale property — under the risk-neutral measure Q, discounted asset prices are martingales, which is the mathematical condition that guarantees no-arbitrage pricing.

label: defined via
description: The martingale property is a statement about conditional expected values — E[M_t | F_s] = M_s — so understanding expectation is a prerequisite for understanding martingales.

label: models rates for
description: Mean-reverting models like Vasicek and Hull-White are the standard tools for modelling the short rate — the mean reversion ensures that interest rates stay within realistic bounds and the yield curve can be fitted exactly.

label: modifies
description: Mean reversion adds a restoring drift term κ(θ − X) to the Brownian motion framework — without it, Brownian motion drifts without bound, which is unsuitable for quantities like interest rates.

label: state-dependent
description: In the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process the drift coefficient is κ(θ − X), which depends on the current state — when X is above θ the drift is negative, pulling the process back down, and vice versa.

label: foundation of
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dX is the starting point for deriving the Black-Scholes PDE — applying Itô's Lemma and the no-arbitrage condition to this equation produces the pricing formula.

label: decomposed into
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dW splits into a drift term and a diffusion term — the diffusion coefficient b(S) = σS is the function that controls the size of the random component.

label: parameterised by
description: The SDE contains exactly two parameters — drift μ and volatility σ — which fully specify the probabilistic behaviour of the asset price.

label: decomposed into
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dW splits into a drift term and a diffusion term — the drift coefficient a(S) = μS is the function that controls the expected rate of change.

label: uses
description: For processes with constant coefficients (e.g. Brownian motion, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck), the transition density takes the form of a normal distribution whose mean and variance depend on the current state and time interval.

label: specialises
description: The transition probability density is a conditional probability density function — it gives the PDF of the future state conditioned on the current state, inheriting all PDF properties including normalisation.

label: simplest case
description: Brownian motion provides the simplest non-trivial transition density — a Gaussian whose variance grows linearly with the time interval, serving as the building block for more complex process densities.

label: solved by
description: The Fokker-Planck equation is the forward PDE that the transition probability density satisfies — solving it with a delta-function initial condition yields the transition density explicitly.

label: converges to
description: As the step size and time step shrink to zero with the ratio 2α(δy)²/δt held constant, the trinomial random walk converges in distribution to Brownian motion — the three-outcome structure provides a richer discrete approximation than the binomial walk.

label: determines
description: The diffusion coefficient D emerges from the trinomial walk through the scaling relation D = 2α(δy)²/δt — choosing α, δy, and δt to satisfy this constraint ensures the discrete variance matches the continuous diffusion rate.

label: generalises
description: The trinomial walk extends the two-outcome discrete random walk by adding a stationary option at each step — this extra degree of freedom allows independent control of the step size and the transition probability, improving lattice convergence.

label: derives
description: Taylor-expanding the trinomial transition probabilities and taking the continuum limit yields the Fokker-Planck equation ∂p/∂t = (D/2)∂²p/∂y² — the trinomial framework is the most direct discrete route to this PDE.

label: notation for
description: The discrete expected value E[X] = Σ xᵢ P(X = xᵢ) is a direct application of summation notation — sigma notation compactly expresses the probability-weighted sum over all outcomes.

label: notation for
description: Variance Var(X) = Σ (xᵢ − μ)² P(X = xᵢ) uses summation notation to express the expected squared deviation — understanding index variables and bounds is essential for computing sample and population variance.

label: notation for
description: Portfolio return E[Rₚ] = Σ wᵢ μᵢ and portfolio variance σₚ² = ΣΣ wᵢ wⱼ σᵢⱼ use single and double summation notation — Markowitz optimisation is expressed entirely in terms of these sums.

label: discrete analogue of
description: Summation is the discrete counterpart of integration — Riemann integration is defined as the limit of finite sums Σ f(xᵢ) Δx as Δx → 0, making summation notation the conceptual bridge to continuous calculus.

label: notation for
description: The binomial coefficient C(n,k) = n\! / (k\!(n−k)\!) uses factorial, which is defined via product notation Π — counting the number of paths with exactly k successes is central to the binomial distribution.

label: notation for
description: For independent random variables the MGF factorises as M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) · M_Y(t) — product notation compactly expresses the factorisation M_{Σ Xᵢ}(t) = Π M_{Xᵢ}(t) that is key to proving the CLT.

label: counterpart of
description: Product notation Π is the multiplicative counterpart of summation notation Σ — taking logarithms converts products to sums (log Π aᵢ = Σ log aᵢ), a technique used throughout statistics and finance.

label: notation for
description: Bayes’ theorem P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B) is built on conditional probability P(A ∩ B)/P(B) — set intersection, complement, and partition notation are essential for stating and applying the theorem correctly.

label: notation for
description: Probability is defined on sets of outcomes within the sample space Ω — the PDF assigns density to events (measurable sets), and expressions like P(X ∈ A) = ∫_A f(x) dx require set notation to specify the domain of integration.

label: foundation of
description: Logical quantifiers operate over sets — ‘for all x ∈ S’ and ‘there exists x ∈ S’ require set membership notation, making set notation the prerequisite for writing and reading quantified mathematical statements.

label: notation for
description: The axioms of a coherent risk measure (monotonicity, sub-additivity, positive homogeneity, translation invariance) are universally quantified statements — e.g. ∀ X,Y: ρ(X+Y) ≤ ρ(X)+ρ(Y) — requiring fluency in ∀ and ⇒ to parse.

label: notation for
description: The martingale property E[Xₜ | Fₛ] = Xₛ is a universally quantified statement that must hold for all s ≤ t — understanding ∀ and conditional statements is essential for grasping what the definition requires.

label: notation for
description: Lemmas are formal mathematical statements that use quantifiers (∀, ∃) and logical connectives (⇒, ⇔) — reading and constructing proofs requires understanding how to negate quantified statements and apply the contrapositive.

label: property of
description: Quadratic variation [W]_t = t is a defining property of Brownian motion — it quantifies the path roughness that distinguishes stochastic from deterministic processes.

label: continuous limit of
description: The discrete random walk has quadratic variation equal to the number of steps (each squared increment is 1) — rescaling to continuous time gives the Brownian motion result [W]_t = t.

label: foundation of
description: Non-zero quadratic variation is the reason the second-order Taylor term survives in stochastic calculus — this directly produces the Itô correction term ½σ²f′′ dt in Itô’s lemma.

label: implies
description: The rule dW² = dt is the infinitesimal form of quadratic variation — [W]_t = t in integral form becomes dW² = dt in differential form.

label: component of
description: The Brownian increment dW ~ N(0, dt) is the infinitesimal building block of Brownian motion — the process is constructed from its independent, normally distributed increments.

label: foundation of
description: Because dW is of order sqrt(dt), its square dW² is of order dt and cannot be ignored — this is the direct cause of the rule dW² = dt.

label: drives
description: The Brownian increment dW is what drives the diffusion term σ S dW in an SDE — its sqrt(dt) scaling means the random component dominates the deterministic drift at short time scales.

label: foundation of
description: The rule dW² = dt is the key substitution in deriving Itô’s lemma — it converts the second-order Taylor term ½ f′′ dW² into the deterministic Itô correction ½ f′′ dt.

label: modifies
description: In ordinary calculus, the second-order Taylor term dx² vanishes; the rule dW² = dt means the stochastic second-order term survives, fundamentally changing how Taylor expansions work for random processes.

label: applies
description: The worked examples apply Itô’s lemma mechanically to power, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions of Brownian motion — building fluency with the formula.

label: derives
description: The exponential example e^{W_t} picking up a +½ dt drift is the origin of the -½σ² correction in the GBM log-return formula — connecting abstract Itô calculus to concrete asset pricing.

label: organises
description: Standard form dG = A dt + B dW_t provides a systematic way to identify drift and diffusion in any SDE, regardless of how the equation is originally written.

label: prerequisite for
description: The Fokker–Planck equation is written directly in terms of the drift A and diffusion B from standard form — you must identify these coefficients before you can write down the transition density PDE.

label: classifies
description: Writing Vasicek and CIR in standard form reveals that they share the same drift structure A = ν − μS but differ in diffusion (constant vs sqrt(S)) — standard form makes model comparison systematic.

label: integral form of
description: Stochastic integrals are Itô’s lemma rearranged into integral form — the dW integral equals the total change in F minus the dt integral, providing a way to evaluate integrals against Brownian motion.

label: produces
description: Itô integrals ∫ g(W) dW are martingales with zero expectation — this property is fundamental to risk-neutral pricing where hedging strategies are expressed as stochastic integrals.

label: integrates against
description: Stochastic integrals are defined as integrals with respect to Brownian motion dW — unlike Riemann integrals, the integrator is a random process with non-zero quadratic variation.

label: technique for
description: The exponential substitution u = e^{αx+βt} v reduces any constant-coefficient linear parabolic PDE to the heat equation — exploiting the fact that the heat equation has known analytical solutions.

label: solves
description: The Black–Scholes PDE is reduced to the heat equation via a change of variables and exponential substitution — this is how the closed-form Black–Scholes formula is derived, producing the N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms.

label: complements
description: Once reduced to the heat equation, the solution is a convolution with the Gaussian kernel — the same self-similar structure studied in similarity solutions, connecting the substitution technique to the fundamental solution.

label: derives
description: Applying Ito s lemma to ln S under GBM produces the -sigma^2/2 correction in the drift — this is exactly the volatility drag, showing that compound growth is lower than the arithmetic mean return.

label: explains
description: Volatility drag explains why the median GBM outcome grows at mu - sigma^2/2 rather than mu — higher volatility lowers the compound growth rate even though the expected value still grows at mu.

label: foundation of
description: Jensen s inequality for the concave log function gives E[ln X] <= ln E[X], which is the mathematical basis for volatility drag — randomness always reduces the compound growth rate.

label: implies
description: The volatility drag correction shows that log-prices drift at mu - sigma^2/2, making log S normally distributed and hence S lognormally distributed.

label: discretises
description: The Euler-Maruyama method converts a continuous-time SDE into a discrete stepping rule by replacing dt with delta t and dW with phi*sqrt(delta t), enabling computer simulation of any SDE.

label: enables
description: Euler-Maruyama discretisation is the core engine of Monte Carlo simulation for SDEs — it provides the time-stepping rule that generates each sample path.

label: applied to
description: Euler-Maruyama applied to GBM gives S_{i+1} = S_i(1 + mu*dt + sigma*phi*sqrt(dt)), though GBM also has an exact exponential formula that avoids discretisation error.

label: applied to
description: Euler-Maruyama applied to the Vasicek model gives r_{i+1} = r_i + gamma(r_bar - r_i)*dt + sigma*phi*sqrt(dt), the standard recipe for simulating mean-reverting interest rates.

label: input to
description: Every Euler-Maruyama step requires a draw from N(0,1), which is generated via inverse transform sampling — applying the inverse normal CDF to a uniform random number.

label: extends
description: Correlated Brownian motions extend single Brownian motion to multiple linked processes with E[dW1 dW2] = rho dt, enabling multi-asset modelling.

label: uses
description: The correlation coefficient rho parameterises how two Brownian motions move together, directly importing the statistical concept of correlation into stochastic calculus.

label: enables
description: Cholesky decomposition transforms independent normal draws into correlated ones with exactly the right correlation structure — it is the standard method for simulating correlated Brownian motions.

label: required for
description: Correlated Brownian motions produce the cross term dS1 dS2 = rho sigma1 sigma2 S1 S2 dt that appears in the multi-asset Ito s lemma.

label: extends
description: The multi-asset Ito s lemma extends the single-asset version by adding cross terms dS1 dS2 that capture the interaction between correlated assets.

label: generalises
description: Applying the multi-asset Ito s lemma to a portfolio of correlated assets leads to the multi-dimensional Black-Scholes PDE, which prices basket and spread options.

label: constructs
description: The zero-drift condition finds a transformation g(S) that eliminates the drift, making g(S) a martingale — a process where today s value is the best forecast of the future.

label: applied in
description: Ito s lemma is applied to g(S) and the drift terms are set to zero, producing the ODE B g  + (1/2) A^2 g   = 0 that defines the zero-drift condition.

label: foundation of
description: The zero-drift condition is the mathematical mechanism behind risk-neutral pricing — by finding the measure under which discounted prices are martingales (zero drift), derivatives can be priced as expected values.

label: specialises
description: The Fokker-Planck steady state is the time-independent solution of the Fokker-Planck equation, obtained by setting the time derivative to zero for a mean-reverting process.

label: characterises
description: The steady-state density exists only for mean-reverting processes — it characterises the long-run distribution that rates or volatilities fluctuate around.

label: recovers
description: The inverse Fokker-Planck problem recovers the drift function from an observed steady-state density — a model-free calibration approach.

label: example of
description: The CIR model is a mean-reverting short-rate model with the same drift structure gamma(r_bar - r) as the Vasicek model but with level-dependent volatility sigma*sqrt(r).

label: state-dependent
description: The CIR diffusion sigma*sqrt(r) depends on the current rate level — as r approaches zero the noise vanishes, preventing negative rates when the Feller condition is met.

label: contrasts with
description: Both CIR and GBM have level-dependent diffusion, but CIR uses sqrt(r) (keeping rates non-negative) while GBM uses S (keeping prices positive), and CIR is mean-reverting while GBM is not.

label: inverts
description: Inverse transform sampling works by inverting the CDF: if U ~ Uniform(0,1), then F^{-1}(U) has distribution F. The CDF is the key ingredient that makes this method exact.

label: uses
description: Inverse transform sampling starts from uniform random numbers on [0,1] and transforms them into draws from any target distribution via the inverse CDF.

label: applied to
description: To generate standard normal draws, inverse transform sampling applies the inverse normal CDF (probit function) to uniform random numbers — this is the foundation of all SDE simulation.

label: alternative to
description: The CLT provides a fast alternative to inverse transform sampling: summing 12 uniform draws and subtracting 6 gives an approximate N(0,1) with mean 0, variance 1, but truncated tails.

label: constrains
description: Jensen s inequality constrains the relationship between E[f(X)] and f(E[X]) based on the convexity of f — for convex f the expected value of the function exceeds the function of the expected value.

label: underlies
description: Jensen s inequality explains why diversification works: the portfolio variance (a convex function of weights) is reduced by averaging, and the concavity of utility functions means investors prefer less uncertainty.

label: solves
description: The integrating factor e^{gamma t} is the standard technique for solving the Vasicek/OU SDE analytically, producing the closed-form solution showing exponentially decaying memory of the initial condition.

label: uses
description: The integrating factor method relies on recognising that multiplying by e^{integral P dt} makes the left-hand side an exact derivative, which can then be integrated directly using standard rules.

label: uses
description: The integrating factor is always an exponential function e^{integral P(t) dt}, making properties of the exponential and logarithm essential to the technique.

label: does not solve
description: Unlike the Vasicek model, the CIR model has state-dependent diffusion sigma*sqrt(r) that prevents a simple integrating factor solution — more advanced techniques are needed.
